Title: Towards Dense Object Tracking in a 2D Honeybee Hive
Authors: Katarzyna Bozek, Laetitia Hebert, Alexander S. Mikheyev and Greg J. Stephens
Category (section): Individual Tracking & Re-identification — Markerless Species-Agnostic Individual Re-Identification
Approach (ai): Convolutional Neural Network; U-Net segmentation; recurrent temporal component
Abstract: This study develops a dense object tracking framework for honey bees in a two-dimensional observation hive. The authors combine convolutional neural networks with a honeybee hive model environment to recognize densely packed individuals from raw image data. They adapt a U-Net-style segmentation architecture to estimate bee location and orientation, incorporate temporal information in a recurrent manner, and generate a large annotated dataset using Amazon Mechanical Turk. The dataset includes more than 375,000 labeled bee instances, and the method detects 96% of individuals with near-human-level location and orientation estimation.
Venue name: 2018 IEEE/CVF Conference on Computer Vision and Pattern Recognition
Research problem: honey bee artificial intelligence Convolutional Neural Network
Bee demographic: Dense detection, localization, orientation estimation, and tracking-oriented analysis of honey bees in a 2D hive

Title: Antennal movements can be used as behavioral readout of odor valence in honey bees
Authors: Federico Gascue, Emiliano Marachlian, Milagros Azcueta, Fernando F. Locatelli and Martín Klappenbach
Category (section): Individual Tracking & Re-identification — Markerless Species-Agnostic Individual Re-Identification
Approach (ai): DeepLabCut; markerless pose estimation; deep learning-based body-part tracking
Abstract: This study uses video-based markerless tracking to quantify antennal movements in restrained honey bees during odor stimulation. Apis mellifera pollen foragers were recorded from a top view at 60 FPS, and DeepLabCut was used to track body landmarks including antennal tips, antennal bases, mandibles, and proboscis. The authors showed that bees oriented their antennae toward appetitive odors and away from aversive odors, and that antennal orientation changed with training strength. The study demonstrates that pose tracking of antennal movements provides a graded behavioral readout of odor valence that is more informative than binary proboscis or sting-extension responses.
Research problem: honey bee artificial intelligence Convolutional Neural Network
Bee demographic: Tracking honey bee antennal movements as a behavioral readout of odor valence and olfactory learning

Title: Real-time insect tracking and monitoring with computer vision and deep learning
Authors: Kim Bjerge, Hjalte M. R. Mann and Toke Thomas Høye
Category (section): Individual Tracking & Re-identification — Markerless Species-Agnostic Individual Re-Identification
Approach (ai): Convolutional Neural Network; YOLOv3; Insect Classification and Tracking algorithm
Abstract: This study presents a field-deployable computer-vision system for detecting, classifying, and tracking individual flower-visiting insects in situ. The system uses a Jetson Nano edge-computing platform, YOLOv3-based detection and classification, and an Insect Classification and Tracking algorithm to link detections into individual tracks. Although the study is not honeybee-only, Apis mellifera is included as an experimental class and was the most abundant taxon in the field recordings. Across 98 days, the system detected 2994 insect tracks, and honeybee tracks were classified with 98% precision.
Venue name: Remote Sensing in Ecology and Conservation
Research problem: honey bee artificial intelligence Convolutional Neural Network
Bee demographic: Detecting, classifying, and tracking flower-visiting insects including Apis mellifera
